perturbation.original_text: In the event a legal dispute arises concerning this Agreement, the prevailing Party shall recover all court costs, expenses, and reasonable attorney's fees.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event a legal dispute arises concerning this Agreement, the prevailing Party may recover court costs, expenses, and attorney's fees, subject to judicial discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall recover' to 'may recover, subject to judicial discretion' makes the recovery of costs and fees uncertain, undermining the clear expectation of reimbursement for the prevailing party. This introduces ambiguity about the financial consequences of a breach.
perturbation.law_explanation: While 28 U.S. Code § 1920 outlines which costs can be awarded in federal court, by making the recovery of costs discretionary ('may recover'), the modified text contradicts the intent of ensuring the prevailing party is adequately compensated for legal expenses, as the discretion could be used to deny costs allowed under the law.

Taxation of costs A judge or clerk of any court of the United States may tax as costs the following: (1) Fees of the clerk and marshal; (2) Fees for printed or electronically recorded transcripts necessarily obtained for use in the case; (3) Fees and disbursements for printing and witnesses; (4) Fees for exemplification and the costs of making copies of any materials where the copies are necessarily obtained for use in the case; (5) Docket fees under s
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 28 U.S. Code § 1920 - Taxation of costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1920 - Taxation of costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next A judge or clerk of any court of the United States may tax as costs the following: (1) Fees of the clerk and marshal; (2) Fees for printed or electronically recorded transcripts necessarily obtained for use in the case; (3) Fees and disbursements for printing and witnesses; (4) Fees for exemplification and the costs of making copies of any materials where the copies are necessarily obtained for use in the case; (5) Docket fees under section 1923 of this title ; (6) Compensation of court appointed experts, compensation of interpreters, and salaries, fees, expenses, and costs of special interpretation services under section 1828 of this title . A bill of costs shall be filed in the case and, upon allowance, included in the judgment or decree. (June 25, 1948, ch. 646, 62 Stat. 955 ; Pub. L. 95–539, § 7 , Oct. 28, 1978 , 92 Stat. 2044 ; Pub. L. 110–406, § 6 , Oct. 13, 2008 , 122 Stat. 4292 .) Historical and Revision Notes Based on title 28, U.S.C., 1940 ed., §§ 9a(a) and 830 (R.S. § 983; Mar. 3, 1911, ch. 231, § 5a, as added Jan. 20, 1944, ch. 3, § 1, 58 Stat. 5 ). For distribution of other provisions of section 9a of title 28 , U.S.C., 1940 ed., see table at end of reviser’s notes. Word “may” was substituted for “shall” before “tax as costs,” in view of Rule 54(d) of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, providing for allowance of costs to the prevailing party as of course “unless the court otherwise directs”. Changes were made in phraseology. Editorial Notes Amendments 2008—Par. (2). Pub. L. 110–406, § 6(1) , substituted “for printed or electronically recorded transcripts” for “of the court reporter for all or any part of the stenographic transcript”. Par. (4). Pub. L. 110–406, § 6(2) , substituted “the costs of making copies of any m
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates the prevailing party 'shall recover' costs and fees, while the changed text makes it discretionary by stating they 'may recover, subject to judicial discretion.' This directly contradicts the principle that the prevailing party should be compensated for legal expenses. While 28 U.S. Code § 1920 doesn't explicitly state 'shall' regarding cost recovery, the change introduces an ambiguity that undermines the expectation of reimbursement, potentially allowing a court to deny costs even when they are typically awarded. The change weakens the right to recover costs, introducing an element of uncertainty where the original text provided a clear entitlement. The modified text therefore introduces a contradiction by weakening an established right.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees not to copy, duplicate, disclose or deliver all or any portion of the Confidential Information to a third party or permit any other third party to inspect, copy or duplicate the same except those parties deemed necessary by the undersi gned to evaluate a possible business relationship (including agents, advisors, affiliates, accountants, attorneys, consultants, and lenders).
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient agrees not to, without undue cause, copy, duplicate, disclose or deliver all or any portion of the Confidential Information to a third party or permit any other third party to inspect, copy or duplicate the same except those parties deemed reasonably necessary by the undersigned to evaluate a possible business relationship (including agents, advisors, affiliates, accountants, attorneys, consultants, and lenders).
perturbation.explanation: The phrases 'without undue cause' and 'reasonably necessary' introduce subjective standards. What constitutes 'undue cause' and 'reasonably necessary' is open to interpretation, weakening the prohibition against disclosing confidential information and making enforcement more difficult.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets
perturbation.law_explanation: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 criminalizes the theft of trade secrets. By adding the ambiguous qualifications of 'without undue cause' and 'reasonably necessary', the altered text potentially allows for unauthorized disclosure of trade secrets under the guise of acceptable reasons, hindering the ability to prosecute under this law, as the burden shifts to proving the disclosure was *not* reasonably necessary or was *due* to some allowable cause, thus weakening the protections provided by the statute.

Theft of trade secrets (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret, that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or decep
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1832 - Theft of trade secrets U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) Whoever, with intent to convert a trade secret , that is related to a product or service used in or intended for use in interstate or foreign commerce, to the economic benefit of anyone other than the owner thereof, and intending or knowing that the offense will, injure any owner of that trade secret , knowingly— (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains such information; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transmits, delivers, sends, mails, communicates, or conveys such information; (3) receives, buys, or possesses such information, knowing the same to have been stolen or appropriated, obtained, or converted without authorization; (4) attempts to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3); or (5) conspires with one or more other persons to commit any offense described in paragraphs (1) through (3), and one or more of such persons do any act to effect the object of the conspiracy, shall, except as provided in subsection (b), be fined under this title or imprisoned not more than 10 years, or both. (b) Any organization that commits any offense described in subsection (a) shall be fined not more than the greater of $5,000,000 or 3 times the value of the stolen trade secret to the organization, including expenses for research and design and other costs of reproducing the trade secret that the organization has thereby avoided. (Added Pub. L. 104–294, title I, § 101(a) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3489 ; amended Pub. L. 112–236, § 2 , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1627 ; Pub. L. 114–153, § 3(a)(1) , May 11, 2016 , 130 Stat. 382
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity by adding 'without undue cause' and changing 'necessary' to 'reasonably necessary'. This weakens the original strict prohibition against disclosing confidential information. 18 U.S. Code § 1832, as shown in both snippets, criminalizes the theft of trade secrets, specifically targeting unauthorized appropriation or conveyance of such information. The addition of subjective qualifiers allows for potential unauthorized disclosure under the guise of 'undue cause' or 'reasonably necessary', thereby hindering prosecution under the law because it creates an ambiguous standard, making it difficult to prove the disclosure was truly unauthorized.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient further agrees to be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by parties in the preceding sentence, and that these parties will not use any of the Confidential Information for any reason or purpose other than to evaluate a possible business relationship and not in any way detrimental to LSUK .
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient should endeavor to ensure that parties in the preceding sentence do not use the Confidential Information for any reason or purpose other than to evaluate a possible business relationship and not in any way detrimental to LSUK .
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'agrees to be responsible' with 'should endeavor to ensure' significantly weakens the Recipient's obligation. It changes a strict liability standard to a best-efforts standard, making it harder to hold the Recipient accountable for breaches by third parties.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act provides remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets, which includes unauthorized disclosure or use. By weakening the Recipient's responsibility for third-party breaches, the revised text makes it more difficult to pursue remedies under the UTSA if a third party misuses the confidential information, as the standard shifts from preventing misuse to merely 'endeavoring' to prevent it.
perturbation.justification: The original text imposes a responsibility on the Recipient for breaches by third parties. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'should endeavor to ensure,' creating an Ambiguous Legal Obligation. This shift undermines the enforcement of obligations related to the confidential information, which could be seen as a contradiction. The Law Explanation highlights the Uniform Trade Secrets Act's remedies for misappropriation, and how the altered text makes pursuing remedies more difficult.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall remain in perpetually and upon request, Recipient will promptly return all data and materials furnished by LSUK , or provide written certification of its destruction and destroy any internal analyses and/or work papers related to these discussions .
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall remain in perpetually unless otherwise stipulated, and upon reasonable request, Recipient will make good faith efforts to return all data and materials furnished by LSUK, or provide a certification of destruction and destroy internal analyses and work papers related to these discussions.
perturbation.explanation: Adding "unless otherwise stipulated" introduces an exception that allows for premature termination without specifying under what conditions this may occur, and also changes 'promptly return' to 'make good faith efforts to return', 'written certification' to 'a certification', and omits the term 'and/or'. This introduces ambiguity about the duration of the agreement and the diligence required for returning or destroying confidential information. It creates uncertainty and diminishes the obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA) - 17 U.S. Code § 1202
perturbation.law_explanation: 17 U.S. Code § 1202 addresses the protection of copyright management information. By allowing premature termination of the agreement and weakening the requirement to promptly return or destroy materials, the modified text increases the risk that copyright management information associated with LSUK's materials could be improperly retained or disclosed, potentially violating the DMCA if this information is subsequently removed or altered.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 U.S. Code § 1202 - Integrity of copyright management information | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 17 U.S. Code § 1202 - Integrity of copyright management information U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) False Copyright Management Information.— No person shall knowingly and with the intent to induce, enable, facilitate, or conceal infringement— (1) provide copyright management information that is false, or (2) distribute or import for distribution copyright management information that is false. (b) Removal or Alteration of Copyright Management Information.— No person shall, without the authority of the copyright owner or the law— (1) intentionally remove or alter any copyright management information , (2) distribute or import for distribution copyright management information knowing that the copyright management information has been removed or altered without authority of the copyright owner or the law, or (3) distribute, import for distribution, or publicly perform works, copies of works, or phonorecords, knowing that copyright management information has been removed or altered without authority of the copyright owner or the law, knowing, or, with respect to civil remedies under section 1203, having reasonable grounds to know, that it will induce, enable, facilitate, or conceal an infringement of any right under this title. (c) Definition.— As used in this section, the term “ copyright management information ” means any of the following information conveyed in connection with copies or phonorecords of a work or performances or displays of a work, including in digital form, except that such term does not include any personally identifying information about a user of a work or of a copy, phonorecord, performance, or display of a work: (1) The title and other information identifying the work, including the information set forth on a notice of copyright. (2) The name of, and o
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the agreement's duration and the diligence required for returning or destroying confidential information. The original text states the agreement remains 'in perpetually' whereas the modified text adds 'unless otherwise stipulated' which undermines the agreement's original intent by allowing for premature termination without specifying conditions. Additionally, the change from 'promptly return' to 'make good faith efforts to return' weakens the obligation. According to the Law Explanation provided, this increases the risk that copyright management information associated with LSUK's materials could be improperly retained or disclosed, potentially violating the DMCA if this information is subsequently removed or altered. The original text has a legal obligation, and the new text weakens the obligation. This aligns with the definition of Ambiguity Legal Contradiction, where a legal statement is made vague, leading to multiple interpretations, making it difficult to enforce.

perturbation.original_text: That all information, data, and materials furnished , either orally or otherwise by LSUK shall be considered "Confidential Information ," which includes all technical and non -technical info rmation concerning the past, present, and future business p ractices and/ or plans to include , without limitation, LSUK 's highly proprietary automated print on demand book manufacturing process , its processes, features, functions, performance, components, s ubsystems, use, technology (whether owned or licensed), intellectual property, resources, research, innovations, products or service offerings, strategic partners, techniques or processes, software, patent applications, inventions, trade secrets, designs, drawings, engineering, hardware configuration information, marketing, strategies, or studies and all tangible and intangible property of any kind, whether conveyed in writing or orally by LSUK or its representatives to Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: That all information, data, and materials furnished , either orally or otherwise by LSUK may be considered "Confidential Information ," which generally includes technical and non -technical info rmation concerning the past, present, and future business p ractices and plans, including LSUK 's automated print on demand book manufacturing process , its processes, features, functions, performance, components. Subsystems and use, technology (whether owned or licensed) and intellectual property. The final classification shall be at LSUK's reasonable discretion.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall be considered' to 'may be considered' and adding 'generally includes' weakens the definition of confidential information. Introducing 'The final classification shall be at LSUK's reasonable discretion' gives LSUK the power to unilaterally decide what is confidential, potentially excluding information that would otherwise be protected. This introduces ambiguity and uncertainty about the scope of confidentiality.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Economic Espionage Act of 1996 (18 U.S. Code § 1831)
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act (18 U.S. Code § 1831) protects trade secrets. By making the definition of 'Confidential Information' discretionary and less inclusive, the modified text could prevent certain information that qualifies as a trade secret under the EEA from being protected by the NDA, making it more difficult to pursue legal action under the EEA if that information is misappropriated.

Economic espionage (a) In General .-Whoever, intending or knowing that the offense will benefit any foreign government, foreign instrumentality, or foreign agent, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains a trade secret; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transm
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 18 U.S. Code § 1831 - Economic espionage | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1831 - Economic espionage U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) In General.— Whoever, intending or knowing that the offense will benefit any foreign government, foreign instrumentality , or foreign agent, knowingly— (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains a trade secret ; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transmits, delivers, sends, mails, communicates, or conveys a trade secret ; (3) receives, buys, or possesses a trade secret , knowing the same to have been stolen or appropriated, obtained, or converted without authorization; (4) attempts to commit any offense described in any of paragraphs (1) through (3); or (5) conspires with one or more other persons to commit any offense described in any of paragraphs (1) through (3), and one or more of such persons do any act to effect the object of the conspiracy, shall, except as provided in subsection (b), be fined not more than $5,000,000 or imprisoned not more than 15 years, or both. (b) Organizations.— Any organization that commits any offense described in subsection (a) shall be fined not more than the greater of $10,000,000 or 3 times the value of the stolen trade secret to the organization, including expenses for research and design and other costs of reproducing the trade secret that the organization has thereby avoided. (Added Pub. L. 104–294, title I, § 101(a) , Oct. 11, 1996 , 110 Stat. 3488 ; amended Pub. L. 112–269, § 2 , Jan. 14, 2013 , 126 Stat. 2442 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2013—Subsec. (a). Pub. L. 112–269, § 2(a) , substituted “not more than $5,000,000” for “not more than $500,000” in concluding provisions. Subsec. (b). Pub. L. 112–2
perturbation.justification: The original text states that information 'shall be considered' confidential, whereas the changed text weakens this to 'may be considered' and adds a discretionary clause that 'The final classification shall be at LSUK's reasonable discretion'. This creates ambiguity regarding what constitutes a trade secret, which the Law Explanation indicates could hinder legal action under the Economic Espionage Act (EEA) if the information is misappropriated. Scraped Law Snippet 2 from 18 U.S. Code § 1831, defines economic espionage as including the unauthorized stealing, copying, or receiving of a 'trade secret'. By making the determination of 'Confidential Information' discretionary, the changed text directly undermines the protection of trade secrets and the ability to prosecute economic espionage under 18 U.S. Code § 1831, as the definition of what constitutes a trade secret becomes subjective and controlled by LSUK, thereby creating a clear legal contradiction.
